Abstract
Organic materials that exhibit gradient conductance plasticity are currently regarded as promising candidates
to play the key role of bionic synapses in flexible neuromorphic circuits, mimicking brain-like processing
behavior. However, owing to their poor long-term operating stability, low intrinsic conductivity, and inferior
For Review Only
charge transfer ability, their electronic synaptic applications are largely restricted. Herein, we controllably
synthesized a two-dimensional (2D) flake-like hydrogen-bonded organic framework composed of meso-
tetra(carboxyphenyl)porphyrin monomers (TCPP-HOF), which serves as a reliable synaptic medium with
high material stability and enhanced charge-carrier transportation. An all-solution-processed 10×10 2D
MXene/TCPP-HOF/MXene/polyimine (PI) heterostructured flexible device array using MXene as the
electrode and PI as the substrate is fabricated, presenting gradient conductance modulation under both
continuous electrical scanning and pulse algorithms, closely simulating biological synaptic behaviors. More
importantly, such a metal-electrode-free device is easily degradable, giving the possibility of recyclable
transient electronics and information security. This work sets a precedent for the development of highly viable
HOF-manipulated artificial synaptic mimicry for low-cost, easily processed, and highly efficient
neuromorphic applications.
https://mc04.manuscriptcentral.com/ijem-caep
Accepted Manuscript
International Journal of Extreme Manufacturing
Page 2 of 19
Keywords: hydrogen-bonded organic frameworks, all-solution-processable devices, artificial synapses,
transient electronics, neuromorphic systems
1. Introduction
Flexible neuromorphic electronic devices for intelligent wearable systems have attracted great attention
because of their advantages of energy efficiency, high speed, and in-memory computing capacity [1-3]. In the
pursuit of hardware neural networks, artificial devices that fully mimic biological synapses are vital. Currently,
prototypes of electronic devices with different structures, such as ferroelectric transistors, complementary
metal-oxide semiconductor (CMOS) transistors, and phase change devices, have shown promise for
simulating the biological neural network of synapses [4-7]. Among them, two-terminal memristive devices
outperform due to their simple structures, inherent adjustable multiple resistances, and memory capabilities,
which are similar to the variable synaptic weights between presynaptic and postsynaptic neurons [8]. So far,
remarkable achievements have been made in bionic memristive synapses, mimicking the basic functions,
including short-term plasticity (STP), long-term plasticity (LTP), and spike-timing-dependent plasticity
(STDP) [9].
Currently, the synaptic devices face numerous challenges, such as high cost, complex fabrication processes,
and intensive energy consumption, which arise from two main aspects: (1) the active layers, such as metal
oxides and two-dimensional (2D) materials, usually require physical and/or chemical vapor deposition,
making large-scale production extremely difficult and expensive; (2) for the active layers that can be prepared
via solution processing (e.g., organic and organic–inorganic hybrid materials), the device electrodes still rely
on vacuum deposition techniques, resulting in high cost and energy dissipation [9-13]. In addition, most
existing synaptic devices are non-degradable, resulting in electronic pollution and resource waste. These
challenges seriously hinder the development of synaptic devices and pose major obstacles to green electronics
[14-18]. Therefore, the construction of all-solution-processed and degradable transient synaptic devices is
For Review Only
vital. To this end, we propose an innovative comprehensive solution of employing solution-processable
organic semiconductors as the active layer, highly conductive MXenes as the electrodes, and easily degradable
self-supported polyimine (PI) films as the substrates, which eliminates the reliance on traditional high-energy-
consuming fabrication methods and provides a strong guarantee for the environmental friendliness of
intelligent devices.
In this contribution, we prepared a tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin-based hydrogen-bonded organic
framework (TCPP-HOF) as the organic active layer and fabricated a 10×10 2D MXene/TCPP-HOF/MXene/PI
synaptic emulator. The MXene-Ti3C2Tx array is inkjet-printed as top and bottom electrodes, whereas the
TCPP-HOF is spin-coated between the electrodes as a switching element. All the device fabrication
procedures are solution processable, which guarantees low cost, scalability, and ease of operation. The device
exhibits gradient conductance modulation with an ultrafast switching speed of 26 ns under a continuous
voltage scanning and pulse algorithm, thus approximating the essential synaptic functions and implementing
https://mc04.manuscriptcentral.com/ijem-caep
Accepted Manuscript
Page 3 of 19
International Journal of Extreme Manufacturing
highly efficient HOF-based neuromorphic application. In addition, this device can rapidly degrade in the
monomer solution of the PI film, especially under heated conditions, ensuring information security in special
scenarios. Moreover, such a bionic device demonstrates convolutional neural network (CNN)-based
computing with an exalted digit recognition accuracy of up to 97.23%, providing a promising paradigm for
the development of green, easy-to-process, and highly efficient neuromorphic computing systems.
2. Results and Discussion
The hydrogen-bonded porphyrin framework (TCPP-HOF) was synthesized by self-assembly of 5,10,15,20-
tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin (TCPP) in a mixed solvent of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and 1,2,4-
trichlorobenzene (TCB) at 140 °C, as schematically illustrated in Figure 1(a) [19-21]. The theoretical
molecular simulation revealed the formation of a square-layered topological TCPP-HOF structure (Figure
1(b)). Moreover, the PI film was synthesized via a condensation reaction. Following the dissolution of p-
phthalaldehyde (0.02 mol) in DMF (50 mL), diethylenetriamine (0.6 mmol) was added with stirring. After
thorough mixing, tris(2-aminoethyl)amine (9.4 mmol) was added dropwise. The solution was promptly
transferred into a mold made of silicone-coated release paper. The film was cured through a step-wise room
temperature to 80 °C thermal treatment, followed by a heat-pressing operation (Figure 1(c)) [22]. After
complete evaporation of the volatiles, a transparent yellow PI film was obtained, serving as a flexible and
degradable substrate.

For Review Only
In light of the all-solution-processable design, we fabricated a 10×10 TCPP-HOF-based synaptic device
array using highly conductive MXenes as the top/bottom electrodes and a degradable PI film as the substrate,
affording a 2D MXene/TCPP-HOF/MXene/PI stacked structure (Figure 2(a)). Firstly, a PI film substrate (2.0
cm × 2.0 cm) was pre-heated on a hotplate at 50 °C. A solution of MXene-Ti3C2Tx (Figure 2(b)) with a
concentration of 1.0 mg·mL−1 was spray-coated onto the PI film through a strip-shaped mask. Then a TCPP-
HOF solution (1.0 mg·mL−1) was spin-coated onto the MXene electrode surface and dried at 50 °C to form a
uniform active layer. Finally, a second layer of MXene was evenly sprayed and dried, yielding a flexible
device with a 10×10 2D MXene/TCPP-HOF/MXene/PI architecture (Figure 2(c)). Compared with traditional
inorganic devices (e.g., metal oxide synapses) requiring high-vacuum and energy-intensive deposition, this
all-solution-processed strategy not only simplifies the fabrication procedure but also enables low-cost
manufacturing, providing a scalable route for green and printable electronics.

The dynamic covalent chemistry of the PI substrate enables a straightforward degradation solution that
tackles electronic waste while creating opportunities for transient electronics. The degradation process is
For Review Only
depicted in Figure 2(d). Immersing the device in a solution containing diethylenetriamine and tris(2-
aminoethyl)amine under heating leads to complete decomposition into the soluble monomeric constituents of
PI within 6 minutes, with any inert residues settling out [22]. This approach underscores the potential of
dynamic covalent polymeric films in creating sustainable flexible electronics.
The synaptic plasticity of the MXene/TCPP-HOF/MXene/PI device was systematically evaluated to
investigate the characteristic responses under a variety of stimuli, including the key functions of long-term
potentiation (LTP), long-term depression (LTD), paired-pulse facilitation (PPF), and post-tetanic potentiation
(PTP) [23-25]. The electrical characteristics of the synaptic device were measured through typical current–
voltage (I–V). sweeps, which were recorded by voltage scanning between −1.0 V and +1.0 V. Figures 3(a)
and S1 illustrate the cyclic I–V characteristic curves when a moderate voltage sweep path (0 → 1.0 → 0→
−1.0 → 0 V) was applied. From the 1st cycle to the 38th cycle, the TCPP-HOF device demonstrates gradient
https://mc04.manuscriptcentral.com/ijem-caep
Accepted Manuscript
International Journal of Extreme Manufacturing
Page 6 of 19
conductance under continuous positive and negative voltage scans, indicating an accumulated resistance
change [26]. Notably, the device maintains its I–V characteristics under heating at 120 °C and affords batch-
to-batch uniformity (Figure S1(b)–(d)), indicating satisfactory thermal stability.
When subjected to successive voltage stimuli, the artificial synapse exhibits a gradual time-dependent
conductance change (ΔWi; Figure 3(b) and (c)), which is characterized by a monotonic current increase of
more than 600 s during continuous voltage sweeps at both polarities (Figure 3(d)). The device achieves stable
and graded conductance modulation, which is essential for emulating the progressive variation in synaptic
strength [27-28]. Notably, the temporal evolution of conductance, controlled by the cumulative voltage
stimulus, can effectively mimic dynamic synaptic weight updates [26, 29]. Furthermore, the device
demonstrates LTP under consecutive pulse trains. Figure 3(e) and (f) display the corresponding conductance
evolution under continuous pulses with amplitudes of 1.0 V and −1.0 V, respectively (width: 203 ms; interval:
309 ms). This behavior mimics a synaptic reinforcement mechanism, wherein the repeated potentiation under
a constant learning duration strengthens memory retention [30-32]. To further detect LTP, the synaptic device
was subjected to extended pulse training. The cumulative influence of programming voltage pulses was
examined by varying the number of applied pulses from 0 to 180 [26, 33-34]. Figure 3(g) shows the resulting
ten conductance states, read at a 0.1 V bias after programming with 1.0 V pulses. These states are distinctly
represented as “0–9” levels, demonstrating the ability to achieve nonvolatile conductance states [35]. Such
long-term memory (LTM) characteristics highlight the device’s substantial promise for high-density
information storage applications.
To emulate synaptic plasticity, we characterized the conductance dynamics under transient pulse stimuli of
varying amplitudes, frequencies, and durations to identify the optimal synaptic-mimicking parameters [33].
The positive pulse trains induce an increase in the transient current across different amplitudes, widths, and
intervals (Figure S2), a similar trend observed under negative pulse trains (Figure S3). Consequently, the
device emulates a biological synaptic response when stimulated by programmed voltage spikes. Through
For Review Only
systematic modulation, the optimal postsynaptic current (PSC) responses are achieved with a positive pulse
(amplitude: 8.0 V; width: 1 μs; interval: 2 μs; Figure 3(h)) and a negative pulse (amplitude: −11.0 V; width:
1 μs; interval: 9 μs; Figure 3(i)). Central to the assessment of the device’s short-term plasticity (STP) are the
phenomena of the PPF and PTP. Their indices, calculated via Equations (S1) and (S2) detailed in Note S1, are
functions of PSCs (I1, I2, and I10) triggered by the initial, second, and tenth pulses [26, 33, 36]. PPF is observed
as a progressive amplification of the PSC from the first to the second paired pulse. The efficacy of this
facilitation, encapsulated by the ΔWPPF value, exhibits an inverse relationship with the inter-pulse interval
(Δt), as shown in Figure 3(j) [36,37]. An analogous decay is noted for the ΔWPTP metric with increasing Δt.
Increasing these indices requires decreasing Δt or increasing the pulse number, suggesting that the temporal
proximity of stimuli intensifies learning efficacy. The asymptotic behavior of ΔWPPF and ΔWPTP at large
Δt values denotes a transition from short-term modulation to a stable state [38].
https://mc04.manuscriptcentral.com/ijem-caep
Accepted Manuscript
Page 7 of 19
International Journal of Extreme Manufacturing
Furthermore, this device demonstrates the ability to emulate another brain-inspired function of prepulse
inhibition (PPI) [26, 39]. In biological synapses, an initial stimulus can suppress responses to subsequent inputs,
enabling the central nervous system to prioritize temporally salient signals. To assess this, four distinct pulse
sequences (P1−P4) were applied to the device (Figure S4). The results show that a weak preceding pulse
effectively inhibits the response to stronger subsequent pulses. The inhibition efficiency (η) was quantified as
η = (Ib – Ia)/Ia × 100%, where Ia and Ib denote the current responses to the first pulse in P1 and the final pulse
in each sequence, respectively. As illustrated in Figure 3(k), this PPI behavior mimics a neurobiological
mechanism for alerting to potential hazards within a given time window, analogous to certain memory-based
warning functions in the human brain.
Moreover, as summarized in Figure 3(l) and Table S1, our device outperforms state-of-the-art HOF-based
synaptic devices across key metrics: a faster response speed of 26 ns (Figure S5), a greater number of ten
conductance states, and a superior convolutional neural network (CNN)-based image recognition accuracy of
97.23% [26, 40-43]. The ultrafast switching speed of 26 ns can be attributed to efficient charge-carrier hopping
within the highly oriented 2D HOF framework. This rapid response, combined with multiple stable
conductance states, allows the device to process high-frequency signals with minimal latency, closely
mimicking the dynamic weight tuning of biological synapses. These results collectively demonstrate the
exceptional switching characteristics of TCPP-HOF-based device and significant potential for neuromorphic
computing applications.

To gain insight into the mechanism behind the synaptic behaviors, we performed detailed structural
characterization and theoretical calculations. The prepared TCPP-HOF material was characterized using
scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and X-ray diffraction (XRD).
https://mc04.manuscriptcentral.com/ijem-caep
Accepted Manuscript
Page 9 of 19
International Journal of Extreme Manufacturing
SEM imaging reveals the successful formation of sheet-like TCPP-HOF structures through the self-assembly
process (Figure 4(a)). TEM measurement indicates that the TCPP-HOF has a nanoplate-like structure (Figure
4(b)). Moreover, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image manifests distinct lattice fringes with a lattice
spacing of 0.327 nm. These results suggest that the ordered nanoplate-like structure of TCPP-HOF can be
attributed to intermolecular interactions within its conjugated framework [44-46]. The characteristic small-
angle XRD peaks of TCPP-HOF confirm the successful formation of the target structure (Figures 4(c) and S6)
[19]. Notably, the π‒π stacking distance determined by XRD is 0.349 nm, which is in accordance with the
observed lattice spacing determined by HRTEM. Furthermore, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and Raman
spectroscopy collectively confirmed the successful synthesis of TCPP-HOF. FTIR analysis shows that TCPP-
HOF has porphyrin fingerprint peaks at 1 730 cm−1, 1 570 cm−1, 1 450 cm−1, 1 330 cm−1, 1 190 cm−1 and 720
cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching vibrations of carboxyl C=O, phenyl C=C, pyrrole C‒N, carboxyl
C‒OH, imino N‒H and pyrrole ring deformation, respectively (Figure 4(d)). The Raman spectrum in Figure
4(e) indicates that TCPP-HOF has characteristic vibration bands of porphyrin at 1 000 cm−1 (pyrrole C‒H), 1
240 cm−1 (carboxyl C‒OH), 1 320 cm−1 (pyrrole C‒N), 1 470 cm−1 (C‒C), and 1 560 cm−1 (phenyl C=C).
Since small molecular porphyrins tend to aggregate through intermolecular π‒π interactions, we
incorporated these functional moieties into the porous framework. Individual porphyrin units are connected
to four adjacent units through O‒H···O hydrogen bonds via carboxyl groups. Adjacent layers are stacked in
the AA mode, with an interlayer spacing of 3.49 Å (Figure 4(f)). The porphyrin moiety in TCPP-HOF is
determined to be non-planar and slightly bent [21]. In biological neural systems, neurotransmitters bridge
neurons by crossing the synaptic cleft to transmit electrical signals from the presynaptic terminal to the
postsynaptic terminal [46-47]. This process can be emulated in our synaptic device. Like neurotransmitters
modulate postsynaptic potential by altering ion channel activity to enable signal propagation, the migration
and accumulation of holes within the device alter its conductance, thereby achieving multilevel resistive
switching [33, 46, 48]. To elucidate the underlying operating mechanism, we performed density functional
For Review Only
theory (DFT) calculations to analyze the electrostatic potential (ESP) and frontier molecular orbitals of TCPP-
HOF (Figures 4(g) and S7). The molecular ESP isosurface reveals an open channel (positive ESP, white and
blue) that facilitates charge transport, alongside electron-deficient acceptor sites (negative ESP, red) that act
as trapping centers to impede charge migration. The computational results further indicate that the highest
occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) is primarily localized on the donor moiety, whereas the lowest
unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) is distributed mainly on the acceptor. This spatial separation of electron
density suggests the feasibility of a charge transfer (CT) process between the donor and acceptor. Under an
applied voltage, charge carriers gain sufficient activation energy to be injected from the donor to the acceptor,
forming a conductive pathway and triggering resistive switching. Specifically, as the positive voltage increases,
the electric field-induced CT drives electrons from the donor to the acceptor, where they are trapped. This
leads to electron retention and the establishment of a conductive channel, thereby increasing the conductivity.
Conversely, upon the application of a negative voltage, a charge-detrapping process occurs, restoring the
https://mc04.manuscriptcentral.com/ijem-caep
Accepted Manuscript
International Journal of Extreme Manufacturing
Page 10 of 19
device to its initial high-resistance state. Notably, the hydrogen bond-regulated HOF periodic framework
provides continuous and ordered internal CT pathways. The progressive CT processes among the HOF
framework enable dynamic conductance modulation and gradient plasticity, which are crucial for mimicking
synaptic weight updates in neuromorphic systems.

To decouple the contribution of the active layer from the electrode effects, we fabricated a control device
with conventional electrodes to ascertain whether the synaptic performance originates intrinsically from the
TCPP-HOF material rather than from interfacial hybridization with MXene. The control device features a
platinum (Pt)/TCPP-HOF/indium tin oxide (ITO) structure, with an ITO substrate as the bottom electrode and
thermally evaporated inert Pt as the top electrode. As shown in Figure 5(a), the I–V characteristics of this
control device over 23 consecutive voltage sweeps (0 → 1.0 → 0 → −1.0 → 0 V) exhibit a conductance
modulation trend similar to that of the pristine MXene/TCPP-HOF/MXene device (Figure 3(a)). The optimal
PSC responses are achieved under identical pulse parameters: a positive pulse (8.0 V, 1 μs, 2 μs; Figure 5(b))
and a negative pulse (−11.0 V, 1 μs, 9 μs; Figure S8). In addition, the temporal evolution of the conductance
in the control device closely matches that of the pristine device (Figures 5(c), S9, and S10). These collective
results provide compelling evidence that the observed synaptic functionalities are intrinsic to the TCPP-HOF
material, highlighting its role as a synaptic medium that operates independently of the electrode material.

Moreover, we explored the mechanical flexibility of the MXene/TCPP-HOF/MXene/PI device. The all-
solution-processability and PI substrate endow the device with excellent bendability. To assess the robustness
of this flexibility, we subjected the device to 200 bending cycles (a bending radius of ~5 mm and a bending
angle of ~120°) and subsequently re-evaluated its electrical properties. The cyclic I–V characteristics (0 →
1.0 → 0 → −1.0 → 0 V) over 30 cycles remain largely unchanged after bending, with no significant
performance degradation (Figure 5(d)). The device exhibits stable current levels with monotonic progression
under the applied read voltages (Figure 5(e)) and maintains its responsive behavior under both positive (Figure
5(f)) and negative (Figure 5(g)) pulse trains. The time-dependent conductance under constant spikes (Figure
5(h) and (i)) is consistent with the pristine state. The device flexibility derives from the mechanical toughness
of the self-supporting PI substrate, the hydrogen bonds of the TCPP-HOF active layers, and the solution-
processable MXene electrodes. Unlike metal electrodes, this metal-electrode-free device maintains a stable
in-device charge transport pathway under repeated mechanical deformation. Moreover, the ultrathin and
lightweight PI-based array conformally adheres to human skin or textiles, holding the potential to implement
energy-efficient and wearable intelligent sensing systems.
To evaluate its neuromorphic computing potential, we conducted CNN-based image recognition training
using the TCPP-HOF device, leveraging its inherent synaptic capabilities [49-51]. As illustrated in Figure 6(a),
the input images are first processed by a convolutional layer for feature extraction, followed by a pooling layer
that reduces data dimensionality to mitigate overfitting. After these processing stages, the data are fed into a
fully connected layer to generate the final output. The network, which comprises 1.2×105 synapses derived
For Review Only
from 28×28 pixel MNIST images, features 10 output neurons corresponding to digits “0–9”. Figure 6(b)
depicts the crossbar device array implementing the fully connected layer, where the input and output neurons
are represented by rows and columns, respectively. The synaptic weight values are represented by the device
conductance, as defined in Equation (1).
𝐼𝑥= ∑𝐺𝑥𝑦𝑉𝑦 (1)
In the system, Gxy corresponds to the device conductance at (x, y). The output value of neuron y is denoted
as Vy, and the total input current Ix to neuron x is given by the multiplication of the conductance matrix and
the voltage vector. Each synaptic weight Wi is determined by the difference in conductance Gi between two
synaptic devices according to Equation (2), thereby enabling the representation of both positive and negative
weights [52-54].

Figure 6. Neuromorphic computing based on CNN. (a) CNN architecture for MNIST recognition. (b)
Synaptic device crossbar array. (c) Potentiation and depression of the rigid device. (d) and (g) Recognition
accuracy achieved through software simulation and hardware training for the rigid device (d) and bent device
(g). (e) and (h) Statistics of the weight distribution modulated by voltage pulses for the rigid device (e) and
bent device (h). (f) and (i) Confusion matrices for evaluating the recognition accuracy for all digits “0–9” for
the rigid device (f) and bent device (i).
The crossbar circuit executes the core synaptic operation through a grounded output line, enabling efficient
weighted summation. The synaptic weights (Wi) are then updated via SET and RESET pulses within the
learning algorithm [55-56]. As evidenced in Figure 6(c), the artificial synapse exhibits stable conductance
https://mc04.manuscriptcentral.com/ijem-caep
Accepted Manuscript
International Journal of Extreme Manufacturing
Page 14 of 19
modulation, demonstrating clear potentiation and depression behaviors. The MNIST dataset-based recognition
accuracy comparisons between the software simulation and our device are evaluated (Figure 6(d)). After
iterative training, the network achieves a maximum accuracy of 97.23%, closely approaching the software
baseline of 99.24%. Furthermore, Figure 6(e) illustrates the distribution of the voltage-pulse-modulated
weights; its normal fitting confirms a broad conductance mapping range, manifesting the ability of the device
to emulate software-derived weight matrices. The corresponding confusion matrix in Figure 6(f) reveals strong
consistency between the predicted and actual labels for all digits “0–9”, with each category attaining
recognition accuracies above 93%. To evaluate the effects of the cyclic bending operations on the CNN-based
computing capacity, the MNIST recognition accuracy of the device is re-evaluated after 200 conformal
deformation cycles, which also results in reliable potentiation and depression plasticity (Figure S11). The
MNIST-based training results show no significant change before and after bending, with the maximum value
remaining at 96.33% (Figure 6(g) and (h)). For the confusion matrix of digits “0–9” under bent condition,
each category maintains a faithful recognition accuracy over 90% (Figure 6(i)). These findings highlight the
potential of this all-solution-processable flexible device for enabling large‑scale, high‑density organic synaptic
arrays and integrated neural‑network circuits, paving a viable path toward bionic intelligent systems.
3. Conclusion
In summary, we developed a TCPP-HOF-pioneered all-solution-processable, degradable, and flexible
synaptic emulator. The TCPP-HOF device achieves gradient conductance switching for bio-synaptic
mimicking, including long-term potentiation (LTP), long-term depression (LTD), paired-pulse facilitation
(PPF), and post-tetanic potentiation (PTP), implementing state-of-the-art HOF-based neuromorphic device
with an ultrafast switching speed of 26 ns. The pulse stimulations achieve multilevel resistance states, which
holds the potential for realizing high-density data storage. In addition, its environmentally friendly
characteristics and degradability help to reduce the electronic burden and develop transient electronics.
For Review Only
Moreover, the TCPP-HOF bio-inspired device achieves CNN-based computation with a digit recognition
accuracy as high as 97.23%, which provides a new direction for the development of highly efficient organic
intelligent sensing, cognitive computing, and neuromorphic systems.
